zh:
冰原没有一本统一的创世书。加拿大、格陵兰与阿拉斯加各地因纽特社群以不同语言、不同名字讲述海兽之母、天气与气息、潜伏在冰下的劫掠者；同一个故事越过海峡，人物的亲属、结局甚至善恶都会改变。十九世纪末，弗朗茨·博厄斯在巴芬岛记录塞德娜诸说，克努德·拉斯穆森又随第五次图勒远征收集伊格卢利克人的歌、禁忌与萨满叙事，纸页才第一次大规模接住这些在风雪里传递的声音。

这里的世界不是一架由高神操纵的机器，而是一张随时可能失衡的关系网。海豹、熊与人曾能互换形体，名字让死者回到新生儿身上，猎物愿不愿现身取决于猎人是否守约。塞德娜把断指化成鲸、海象与海豹，也会因人的犯忌把兽群扣在海底；安加库克必须在黑暗中下潜，为她梳开发结。风、天气与人的心智同属「西拉」一词，呼吸与世界彼此贯通。所谓怪物也常守着现实边界：冰下的卡卢皮卢克拖走靠近裂水的孩子，伊吉拉克让旅人迷失，影人只在余光中显形。它们不是供陈列的怪兽名册，而是极地生活把危险、伦理与记忆刻进夜色的方式。
en:
The Arctic has no single book of creation. Inuit communities across Canada, Greenland, and Alaska tell of the Sea Woman, the intelligence within weather, and beings beneath the ice in different languages and under different names. As a story crosses a strait, its kinships, endings, and moral weight may change. Franz Boas recorded Baffin Island versions in the late nineteenth century; Knud Rasmussen’s Fifth Thule Expedition later gathered Iglulik songs, taboos, and shamanic accounts, giving a large written afterlife to voices carried through snow and memory.

This world is less a machine ruled by distant gods than a web of relationships forever in danger of imbalance. Seals, bears, and people once exchanged forms; names return the dead through newborn children; game presents itself only when hunters observe the proper obligations. Sedna’s severed fingers become whales, walruses, and seals, yet broken taboos can make her withhold them until an angakkuq descends in darkness to comb her hair. Sila can mean weather, air, and the intelligence that moves through a person: breath and world are continuous. The frightening beings guard practical thresholds. Qallupilluit carry away children who approach open water, ijirait turn travelers from the path, and shadow people appear only at the rim of sight. They are not a cabinet of monsters but the Arctic’s way of cutting danger, duty, and remembrance into the long night.
